content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION

November 12, 1997

Charles G. Abdelnour
City Clerk
City of San Diego
202 C Street
San Diego, California 92101

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-97-500

Dear Mr. Abdelnour:

This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

QUESTIONS

1. May "a person appointed to a public board or commission" in the City of San Diego make contributions to a committee opposing the recall of a San Diego officeholder when the officeholder is "the person who made the appointment" and controls the committee?

2. Does the answer change if the committee is not controlled by the officeholder?

3. Does the publication of a notice of intent to recall play a role in determining the answer?

CONCLUSIONS

1. No. Considering the City of San Diego's treatment of candidates running in a recall election and the Act's well established definitions of "controlled committee" and "contribution," Commission staff reads Section 85705 to prohibit "a person appointed to a public board or

_________________________
¹ Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 - 18995, of the California Code of Regulations.

428 J Street, Suite 450, Sacramento, CA 95814●P. O. Box 807, Sacramento, CA 95812● (916) 322-5660

File No. A-97-500
Page 2

commission" from making a contribution to an elected official who is the subject of a recall in
the City of San Diego, if the officeholder "is the person who made the appointment" and
controls the committee.

2. Yes. If the officeholder does not control the committee opposing the recall, then
Section 85705 does not apply, and the "person appointed to the public board or commission"
may make a contribution to the committee formed to oppose the recall of the officeholder "who
made the appointment."

3. No.

FACTS

The City of San Diego is a charter city. It has a local campaign finance ordinance
codified in the San Diego Municipal Code. The definition of "measure" expressly states that a
measure does not include a recall election. (SDMC § 27.2903(k).) In addition, the definition of
"candidate" includes a city official who becomes the subject of a recall election. (SDMC §
27.2903(b)(4).) The municipal code also contains contribution limits of $250 per person for any
single election. (SDMC § 27.2941.) The recall election begins with publication of a notice of
intent to recall an elected official. Presently, four committees are raising or have raised funds to
support or oppose a recall effort against two councilmembers, although no notice of intent to
recall either of these councilmembers has yet been published.

ANALYSIS

Under the Act, a recall election is considered to be a ballot measure under the Act and
candidates running in a recall election are not subject to contribution limitations. (Cohen Advice
Letter, No. I-96-364; Roberts Advice Letter, No. A-97-173.) we concluded that state law does not preempt the City of San Diego's
decision to limit contributions to candidates in recall elections. Because of San Diego's decision
to treat recall elections like any other candidate election, issues related to recall elections in San
Diego must necessarily be treated uniquely.

Section 85705, added by Proposition 208, provides:

"No person appointed to a public board or commission or as
Trustee of the California State University or Regent of the
University of California during tenure in office shall donate to,
solicit or accept any contribution for, any committee controlled by
the person who made the appointment to that office or any other
entity with the intent that the recipient of the donation be any
committee controlled by such person who made the appointment."

File No. A-97-500
Page 3

Proposition 208 did not change whether the City of San Diego could limit contributions
to candidates running in a recall election. Neither did Proposition 208 amend the definition of
"contribution" in Section 82015. Finally, Proposition 208 did not amend the definition of a
controlled committee in Section 82016. A controlled committee is defined in the Act as:

"[A] committee which is controlled directly or indirectly by a
candidate or state measure proponent or which acts jointly with a
candidate, controlled committee or state measure proponent in
connection with the making of expenditures. A candidate or state
measure proponent controls a committee if he, his agent or any
other committee he controls has a significant influence on the
actions or decisions of the committee."

A contribution is "any payment made for political purposes for which full and adequate
consideration is not made to the donor." A payment is made for "political purposes" if, among
other things, a payment is received by a candidate or a controlled committee. (Regulation
18215.)

Section 85202, added by Proposition 208, states:

"Unless specifically superseded by this act, the definitions and
provisions of this title shall govern the interpretation of this law."

Considering the City of San Diego's treatment of candidates running in a recall election
and the Act's well established definitions of "controlled committee" and "contribution,"
Commission staff reads Section 85705 to prohibit "a person appointed to a public board or
commission" from making a contribution to an elected official who is the subject of a recall in
the City of San Diego, if the officeholder "is the person who made the appointment" and
controls the committee. However, if the officeholder does not control the committee to support
or oppose the recall, then Section 85705, by its own terms, would not apply. In addition, the
publication of a notice of intent to recall does not play a role in determining whether the
provisions of Section 85705 apply. If applicable, however, all the reporting and other
requirements of the Act apply.

File No. A-97-500
Page 4

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-
5660.

Sincerely,

Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel

[signature]

By:    Marte Castaños
       Staff Counsel, Legal Division

answer?

connection with the making of expenditures. A candidate or state
